content: Topical fluoride promotes remineralization of early enamel lesions by driving calcium and phosphate ions back into demineralized enamel, forming fluorapatite, which is more acid-resistant than the original hydroxyapatite.
title: Fluoride and the caries process: a contemporary review

content: Fluoride in plaque and saliva inhibits demineralization at the crystal surface and interferes with bacterial enzyme activity, reducing acid production by cariogenic bacteria such as Streptococcus mutans.
title: Mechanisms of action of fluoride in caries prevention

content: Community water fluoridation is associated with a median reduction of 25% in dental caries across the lifespan in populations studied, when compared with non-fluoridated communities.
title: Water fluoridation for the prevention of dental caries

content: Toothpaste containing 1000-1500 ppm fluoride provides a clinically significant caries-preventive effect in children and adults; effect size increases with fluoride concentration and brushing frequency.
title: Fluoride toothpastes of different concentrations for preventing dental caries

content: Root canal treatment is indicated when the dental pulp is irreversibly inflamed (irreversible pulpitis) or necrotic, typically presenting as lingering pain to hot/cold stimuli, spontaneous pain, or signs of apical periodontitis.
title: Diagnosis and management of pulpal and periapical disease

content: Reported success rates for primary root canal treatment, defined as absence of apical periodontitis at follow-up, range from approximately 83% to 93% across systematic reviews, depending on preoperative status and follow-up period.
title: Outcome of primary root canal treatment: systematic review

content: Modern endodontic treatment is routinely performed under local anaesthesia; most patients report pain levels during treatment comparable to a routine restorative procedure, though post-operative discomfort for a few days is common.
title: Pain and anxiety in endodontic treatment: a clinical overview

content: When a tooth is unrestorable or has a hopeless prognosis, extraction followed by prosthetic replacement (implant or bridge) is the alternative to root canal treatment; the decision balances restorability, cost, and patient preference.
title: Retain or extract: decision-making in compromised teeth

content: Sleep bruxism in adolescents is associated with reported prevalence between 10% and 20%, with contributing factors including psychosocial stress and, possibly, sleep-disordered breathing.
title: Bruxism in children and adolescents: an update

content: Management of bruxism is largely aimed at protecting teeth and reducing symptoms; occlusal splints may prevent tooth wear but there is limited evidence that they stop the bruxism activity itself.
title: Management strategies for sleep bruxism

content: A definitive diagnosis of sleep bruxism relies on polysomnography, but clinical diagnosis based on self-report, tooth wear, and masseter hypertrophy is commonly used in practice.
title: Diagnostic criteria for bruxism: a consensus

content: Resin-based pit and fissure sealants reduce the incidence of occlusal caries in the permanent molars of children and adolescents compared with no sealant, with the preventive effect persisting for several years when sealants remain intact.
title: Sealants for preventing dental decay in permanent teeth

content: Sealant retention is a key determinant of effectiveness; periodic clinical review is recommended to detect and repair sealant loss, particularly in the first year after placement.
title: Clinical performance of pit and fissure sealants

content: Sealant placement is a non-invasive procedure that does not require removal of tooth structure; the tooth surface is cleaned, etched, and the resin is applied and light-cured.
title: Technique guide for pit and fissure sealant application

content: Cost-effectiveness analyses suggest sealants are most beneficial for teeth and populations at elevated caries risk; universal sealing of low-risk surfaces yields smaller marginal benefit.
title: Cost-effectiveness of dental sealants: a review

content: A meta-analysis of gamification in education found small-to-moderate positive effects on student engagement and motivation, although effect sizes vary widely with design and context.
title: Gamification in education: a meta-analysis of engagement effects

content: Game elements such as points, badges, and leaderboards can increase participation, but their effect on deep learning outcomes is inconsistent and may fade over time as novelty wears off.
title: Does gamification work? A review of points, badges and leaderboards

content: Poorly designed extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation (the over-justification effect); effective gamification supports learners' autonomy and competence rather than merely rewarding activity.
title: Self-determination theory and the design of gamification

content: In mathematics learning, game-based approaches have been associated with improved achievement and reduced anxiety compared with conventional instruction across several controlled studies.
title: Game-based learning in mathematics: a systematic review

content: On well-defined prompts, automated essay scoring systems can reach agreement with human raters comparable to inter-human agreement, with quadratic weighted kappa around 0.7 to 0.8.
title: Handbook of automated essay evaluation: agreement with human raters

content: Automated essay scoring models rely on surface features and can be gamed by well-structured but vacuous text; they do not understand meaning and may misjudge creative or atypical responses.
title: When the machine scores: limitations of automated essay scoring

content: Automated scoring can exhibit bias across demographic groups and language varieties; fairness audits and human oversight are recommended before any high-stakes use.
title: Fairness in automated essay scoring

content: Best practice positions automated scoring as assistive: a human reviews flagged or borderline essays, combining machine efficiency with human accountability.
title: Human-in-the-loop automated assessment

content: Immersive virtual reality can improve learning of spatial and procedural tasks, but evidence for abstract conceptual learning is mixed and susceptible to novelty effects.
title: A systematic review of immersive VR in education

content: Virtual laboratories can provide safe, low-cost practice of experiments, though they may not fully replace the tactile skills developed in hands-on labs.
title: Learning outcomes of virtual versus hands-on laboratories

content: Dental plaque is a structured bacterial biofilm on tooth surfaces; the bacterial products it releases trigger the gingival inflammation that begins gum disease.
title: Dental plaque biofilm and periodontal disease

content: If plaque is not removed, gingivitis can progress to periodontitis, in which the inflammatory response destroys the periodontal ligament and alveolar bone supporting the teeth.
title: From gingivitis to periodontitis

content: Daily mechanical plaque control - toothbrushing combined with interdental cleaning - is the cornerstone of preventing and controlling gingival inflammation.
title: Mechanical plaque control for gingival health

content: Professional scaling and root planing removes plaque and calculus below the gumline and significantly reduces probing depths and bleeding in patients with periodontitis.
title: Efficacy of scaling and root planing

content: Smoking is a major modifiable risk factor that increases the severity of periodontitis and impairs the response to periodontal treatment.
title: Smoking and periodontal disease

content: Bleeding while brushing is an early sign of gingival inflammation and should not be dismissed as normal; it usually reverses with improved plaque control.
title: Bleeding gums as an early sign of gingivitis

content: Dentin hypersensitivity is a short, sharp pain from exposed dentin in response to stimuli such as cold, heat or sweetness, that cannot be attributed to any other dental defect.
title: Dentin hypersensitivity: consensus definition

content: Sensitivity occurs when enamel loss or gum recession exposes dentinal tubules, allowing fluid movement that stimulates nerves in the pulp - the hydrodynamic theory.
title: Mechanisms of dentin hypersensitivity

content: Aggressive horizontal brushing with a hard brush and abrasive pastes can wear enamel and expose dentin, contributing to sensitivity.
title: Toothbrushing, abrasion and dentine wear

content: Desensitizing toothpastes containing potassium nitrate or stannous fluoride reduce dentin hypersensitivity compared with control in clinical trials.
title: Efficacy of desensitizing toothpastes

content: Reducing acidic foods and drinks and not brushing immediately after acid exposure helps limit enamel erosion and sensitivity.
title: Dietary acids, erosion and sensitivity

content: Persistent or severe sensitivity may signal a cracked tooth, caries or a failing restoration and warrants evaluation by a dentist.
title: When to refer tooth sensitivity

content: In a flipped classroom, direct instruction is delivered before class (often via video) so class time can be used for active, collaborative problem-solving.
title: Defining the flipped classroom

content: Meta-analyses report a small-to-moderate positive effect of flipped classrooms on student achievement compared with traditional lecturing.
title: A meta-analysis of the effects of flipped classrooms

content: Flipped designs increase the share of class time spent on active learning and student-instructor interaction.
title: Active learning in flipped courses

content: Benefits depend on students completing pre-class preparation; accountability mechanisms such as short quizzes improve outcomes.
title: Pre-class preparation and flipped learning

content: Effect sizes vary widely with implementation quality, subject area and education level.
title: Moderators of flipped-learning effectiveness

content: Students often report higher engagement and satisfaction in well-designed flipped courses.
title: Student perceptions of flipped learning

content: The spacing effect: distributing study across multiple sessions produces better long-term retention than the same amount of massed study.
title: The spacing effect: a temporal meta-analysis

content: Retrieval practice - actively recalling information - strengthens memory more than simply restudying it, an effect known as the testing effect.
title: Test-enhanced learning

content: The optimal gap between study sessions grows with the desired retention interval: longer gaps suit information that must be retained longer.
title: Optimizing distributed practice

content: Spaced-repetition software schedules reviews with algorithms (e.g. the Leitner system or SM-2) that expand intervals for well-remembered items.
title: Spaced repetition systems for learning

content: The benefits of spacing and retrieval practice generalize across ages, subjects and materials, making them broadly applicable study strategies.
title: Improving learning with cognitive strategies

content: Interleaving different problem types during spaced practice further improves discrimination and transfer in problem-solving domains.
title: Interleaved practice improves learning
